text_summary: ELIANA (NCT02435849) is the pivotal, single-arm, open-label, multi-center, Phase 2 registration trial of tisagenlecleucel (Kymriah, CTL019, Novartis/UPenn) in pediatric and young adult patients with relapsed or refractory (r/r) CD19-positive B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (B-ALL). The study enrolled patients aged 3-21 years who had failed at least two lines of therapy or relapsed after allogeneic stem cell transplant. The CAR-T construct uses a second-generation design with a CD19-targeting single-chain variable fragment (scFv), a 4-1BB (CD137) costimulatory domain, and a CD3-zeta signaling domain delivered via lentiviral transduction. The primary endpoint was overall remission rate (ORR), defined as complete remission (CR) or CR with incomplete hematologic recovery (CRi) within 3 months of infusion. A total of 75 patients were infused. The ORR was 81% (61/75), with all responding patients achieving minimal residual disease (MRD)-negative status by flow cytometry at a sensitivity of 0.01%. The 12-month event-free survival (EFS) rate was 50% and the 12-month overall survival (OS) rate was 76%. Median duration of response was not reached at primary analysis. Cytokine release syndrome (CRS) occurred in 77% of patients, with 47% experiencing Grade 3 or higher CRS per the Penn grading scale, managed with tocilizumab and corticosteroids. Neurotoxicity (immune effector cell-associated neurotoxicity syndrome, ICANS) occurred in 40% of patients, with 13% Grade 3 or higher. There were no deaths attributed to tisagenlecleucel. B-cell aplasia was observed as an expected on-target, off-tumor effect, managed with immunoglobulin replacement. ELIANA led to the first-ever FDA approval of a CAR-T cell therapy on August 30, 2017, and represented a watershed moment in oncology. Long-term follow-up demonstrated durable responses in a subset of patients, with some maintaining remission beyond 5 years.
outcome_summary: ORR 81% (61/75), all responders MRD-negative. 12-month EFS 50%, 12-month OS 76%. CRS in 77% (47% Grade 3+). Neurotoxicity in 40% (13% Grade 3+). No treatment-related deaths. Led to first FDA approval of CAR-T therapy (August 2017). Durable remissions observed beyond 5 years in a subset of patients.

text_summary: JULIET (NCT02445248) is a pivotal, single-arm, open-label, multi-center, Phase 2 trial evaluating tisagenlecleucel (Kymriah, CTL019) in adult patients with relapsed or refractory (r/r) diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL). The study enrolled patients aged 18 years and older with r/r DLBCL who had received at least two prior lines of therapy, including patients who relapsed after or were ineligible for autologous stem cell transplantation. The CAR construct is second-generation, targeting CD19 via a murine-derived scFv with a 4-1BB costimulatory domain and CD3-zeta activation domain, manufactured using lentiviral transduction at decentralized sites. The primary endpoint was best overall response rate (ORR) by independent review committee (IRC) per Lugano 2014 criteria. A total of 93 evaluable patients were infused. The ORR was 52% (48/93), including a complete response (CR) rate of 40% (37/93) and a partial response (PR) rate of 12%. The median duration of response was not reached for patients achieving CR. The 12-month relapse-free probability among complete responders was 65%. Median OS was 11.1 months for all infused patients, with the 12-month OS rate at 49%. CRS occurred in 58% of patients (22% Grade 3-4 per Penn scale), and neurological events occurred in 21% (12% Grade 3-4). Three patients died from adverse events possibly related to tisagenlecleucel, including CRS-related complications. Bridging chemotherapy was permitted during the manufacturing period, used by 92% of patients. Notably, the decentralized manufacturing model led to longer vein-to-vein times compared to centralized approaches. JULIET supported the FDA approval of Kymriah for r/r DLBCL in May 2018, making it the first CAR-T therapy approved for a lymphoma indication. The trial demonstrated that 4-1BB costimulated CAR-T cells could produce durable remissions in aggressive lymphoma without further consolidative therapy.
outcome_summary: ORR 52%, CR rate 40%. 12-month relapse-free probability 65% for CR patients. Median OS 11.1 months for all infused patients. CRS 58% (22% Grade 3-4). Neurological events 21% (12% Grade 3-4). Three possibly treatment-related deaths. Supported FDA approval for r/r DLBCL (May 2018).

text_summary: ZUMA-1 (NCT02348216) is the pivotal, multi-center, single-arm, Phase 1/2 registration trial of axicabtagene ciloleucel (Yescarta, axi-cel, KTE-C19, Kite Pharma/Gilead) in adult patients with relapsed or refractory (r/r) large B-cell lymphoma, including DLBCL, primary mediastinal large B-cell lymphoma (PMBCL), and transformed follicular lymphoma (TFL). The CAR construct is a second-generation anti-CD19 CAR with a CD28 costimulatory domain and CD3-zeta signaling domain, delivered via retroviral transduction using a centralized manufacturing process at a single facility. Patients aged 18 and older who had failed at least two prior lines of systemic therapy were eligible. Notably, bridging therapy was not permitted between leukapheresis and conditioning chemotherapy. The primary endpoint of Phase 2 was objective response rate (ORR). In the pivotal Phase 2 cohort, 101 patients were treated. The ORR was 83%, with a CR rate of 58%. At 24-month follow-up, the durable response rate was 39%, and the median OS was not reached. The 2-year OS rate was 51%. CRS occurred in 93% of patients (13% Grade 3+), and neurological events occurred in 64% of patients (28% Grade 3+), reflecting the CD28-costimulatory domain's rapid and potent T-cell activation kinetics. The centralized manufacturing process achieved a 99% manufacturing success rate with a median turnaround time of 17 days. Two deaths were attributed to axi-cel (hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis and cardiac arrest). The rapid expansion kinetics of CD28-costimulated CARs produced earlier peak expansion (days 7-14) compared to 4-1BB constructs. ZUMA-1 led to FDA approval of Yescarta in October 2017, making it the second CAR-T product approved in the United States. Five-year follow-up data showed ongoing responses in approximately one-third of patients, establishing axi-cel as a potentially curative therapy for r/r LBCL.
outcome_summary: ORR 83%, CR 58%. 2-year OS 51%, median OS not reached. 5-year durable response rate ~31%. CRS 93% (13% Grade 3+). Neurotoxicity 64% (28% Grade 3+). 99% manufacturing success. Two treatment-related deaths. FDA approval October 2017 for r/r LBCL.

text_summary: ZUMA-3 (NCT02614066) is a pivotal, single-arm, open-label, multi-center, Phase 1/2 trial of brexucabtagene autoleucel (Tecartus, brexu-cel, KTE-X19, Kite Pharma/Gilead) in adult patients with relapsed or refractory (r/r) B-cell precursor acute lymphoblastic leukemia (B-ALL). Unlike axi-cel, brexu-cel incorporates a T-cell enrichment step during manufacturing to deplete circulating leukemic blasts from the leukapheresis product, which is critical in leukemia patients. The CAR construct is second-generation with an anti-CD19 scFv, CD28 costimulatory domain, and CD3-zeta signaling domain. Patients aged 18 and older with r/r B-ALL after at least two prior therapies or relapse after allogeneic transplant were eligible, including those who failed prior blinatumomab or inotuzumab. The Phase 1 portion established the recommended dose. The Phase 2 primary endpoint was overall CR rate (CR + CRi) assessed by independent review. In Phase 2, 55 patients were treated. The overall CR rate was 71% (39/55), with 56% achieving CR and 15% CRi. Among responders, 97% achieved MRD-negativity. Median duration of remission was 14.6 months, and median OS was 25.4 months for all treated patients. The 12-month OS rate was 71%. CRS occurred in 89% (24% Grade 3+), and neurological events in 60% (25% Grade 3+), with patterns consistent with CD28-costimulated CAR-T cells. The median time to CAR-T peak expansion was 15 days. Two deaths occurred due to treatment-related adverse events. ZUMA-3 supported the FDA approval of Tecartus for r/r B-ALL in October 2021. This was notable as the first and only CAR-T therapy approved for adult B-ALL, addressing a significant unmet need in a population with historically poor outcomes after failure of standard salvage therapy.
outcome_summary: CR/CRi rate 71% (39/55), 97% of responders MRD-negative. Median DOR 14.6 months, median OS 25.4 months. CRS 89% (24% Grade 3+), neurotoxicity 60% (25% Grade 3+). Two treatment-related deaths. First CAR-T approved for adult B-ALL (FDA October 2021).

text_summary: ZUMA-7 (NCT03391466) is a landmark, randomized, open-label, Phase 3 trial comparing axicabtagene ciloleucel (Yescarta, axi-cel) to standard of care (SOC; salvage chemotherapy followed by high-dose therapy and autologous stem cell transplant, HDT-ASCT) as second-line therapy for adult patients with relapsed or refractory (r/r) large B-cell lymphoma (LBCL) within 12 months of first-line chemoimmunotherapy. This was the first randomized Phase 3 trial in the CAR-T field and fundamentally changed the treatment paradigm. Patients were randomized 1:1, with 180 patients in the axi-cel arm and 179 in the SOC arm. The primary endpoint was event-free survival (EFS) by blinded central review. The study met its primary endpoint: median EFS was 8.3 months for axi-cel versus 2.0 months for SOC (HR 0.398, p<0.0001). The ORR was 83% for axi-cel versus 50% for SOC, and CR rates were 65% versus 32%, respectively. The 2-year EFS rate was 41% for axi-cel versus 16% for SOC. Notably, only 36% of SOC patients were able to proceed to HDT-ASCT, highlighting the limitations of the standard approach. In an updated analysis, median OS was 47.2 months for axi-cel versus 31.1 months for SOC, though the OS difference did not reach statistical significance at the pre-specified boundary due to crossover. CRS occurred in 92% (6% Grade 3+) and neurological events in 60% (21% Grade 3+) in the axi-cel arm. No new safety signals emerged compared to the third-line setting. ZUMA-7 led to FDA approval of axi-cel as second-line therapy for r/r LBCL in April 2022, moving CAR-T earlier in the treatment algorithm. This trial, along with TRANSFORM and BELINDA, redefined the treatment landscape for relapsed aggressive lymphoma.
outcome_summary: Primary endpoint met: median EFS 8.3 vs 2.0 months (HR 0.398, p<0.0001). ORR 83% vs 50%, CR 65% vs 32%. 2-year EFS 41% vs 16%. Median OS 47.2 vs 31.1 months (not significant at boundary due to crossover). CRS 92% (6% Grade 3+), neurotoxicity 60% (21% Grade 3+). FDA approval for 2nd-line LBCL April 2022.

text_summary: TRANSCEND NHL 001 (NCT02631044) is a pivotal, seamless-design, single-arm, open-label, multi-center, Phase 1/2 trial of lisocabtagene maraleucel (Breyanzi, liso-cel, JCAR017, Bristol-Myers Squibb/Juno Therapeutics) in adult patients with relapsed or refractory (r/r) large B-cell lymphoma (LBCL). Liso-cel is a distinctive second-generation CD19-directed CAR-T product with a 4-1BB costimulatory domain, differentiated by its defined composition: it is administered as a precise 1:1 ratio of CD8+ and CD4+ CAR-T cells at specified dose levels, aiming to reduce variability in efficacy and toxicity. Eligible patients had r/r LBCL including DLBCL NOS, HGBCL, PMBCL, FL Grade 3B, and DLBCL transformed from indolent lymphoma, after at least two prior therapies. The primary endpoint was ORR. In the pivotal efficacy-evaluable population (n=256), the ORR was 73% with a CR rate of 53%. The median duration of response was 16.7 months for all responders and not reached for patients achieving CR. Median PFS was 6.8 months, and median OS was 27.3 months. The safety profile was notably favorable compared to other approved CAR-T products: CRS occurred in 42% (2% Grade 3+) and neurotoxicity in 30% (10% Grade 3+), the lowest severe toxicity rates among the three approved CD19 CAR-T products for LBCL at that time. The defined-composition approach contributed to more predictable pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics. Manufacturing success rate was 97%. Liso-cel received FDA approval for r/r LBCL in February 2021. The favorable safety profile made liso-cel a preferred option for older or frailer patients and supported its subsequent investigation in earlier lines of therapy.
outcome_summary: ORR 73%, CR 53%. Median DOR 16.7 months (not reached for CR). Median PFS 6.8 months, median OS 27.3 months. CRS 42% (2% Grade 3+), neurotoxicity 30% (10% Grade 3+) -- lowest severe toxicity among approved CD19 CARs. 97% manufacturing success. FDA approved February 2021.

text_summary: TRANSFORM (NCT03575351) is a randomized, open-label, Phase 3 trial comparing lisocabtagene maraleucel (Breyanzi, liso-cel) to standard of care (SOC; salvage chemoimmunotherapy followed by HDT-ASCT) as second-line treatment for adult patients with relapsed or refractory (r/r) large B-cell lymphoma (LBCL) that was primary refractory to or relapsed within 12 months of first-line therapy. Patients were randomized 1:1, and the primary endpoint was EFS per IRC. A total of 184 patients were randomized (92 per arm). The trial met its primary endpoint: median EFS was not reached for liso-cel versus 2.3 months for SOC (HR 0.349, p<0.0001). The CR rate was 74% for liso-cel versus 43% for SOC. The 12-month EFS rate was 45% for liso-cel versus 15% for SOC. Only 47% of SOC patients completed the planned transplant pathway. In a post-hoc updated analysis, median OS was not reached for liso-cel versus 29.9 months for SOC (HR 0.72). CRS occurred in 49% (1% Grade 3+) and neurological events in 12% (4% Grade 3+) in the liso-cel arm, maintaining the favorable safety profile seen in TRANSCEND. The low toxicity rates were notable compared to the ZUMA-7 results with axi-cel. TRANSFORM, together with ZUMA-7, provided the evidence base for moving CAR-T therapy to the second-line setting for high-risk LBCL patients. TRANSFORM led to an expanded FDA approval of liso-cel for second-line treatment of r/r LBCL in June 2022. The trial was particularly significant because, unlike BELINDA (which failed), it demonstrated that the defined-composition liso-cel product could produce superior outcomes compared to SOC in a randomized setting.
outcome_summary: Primary endpoint met: median EFS not reached vs 2.3 months for SOC (HR 0.349, p<0.0001). CR 74% vs 43%. 12-month EFS 45% vs 15%. CRS 49% (1% Grade 3+), neurotoxicity 12% (4% Grade 3+). Only 47% of SOC completed transplant. FDA expanded approval for 2nd-line LBCL June 2022.

text_summary: KarMMa (NCT03361748) is the pivotal, single-arm, open-label, multi-center, Phase 2 trial of idecabtagene vicleucel (Abecma, ide-cel, bb2121, Bluebird Bio/Bristol-Myers Squibb) in adult patients with relapsed and refractory multiple myeloma (r/r MM). This was the first registration trial for a BCMA-directed CAR-T therapy. The CAR construct is second-generation with an anti-BCMA scFv, a 4-1BB costimulatory domain, and CD3-zeta signaling domain, manufactured via lentiviral transduction. Patients had received at least 3 prior lines of therapy including an immunomodulatory drug (IMiD), a proteasome inhibitor (PI), and an anti-CD38 antibody, and were refractory to the last regimen. Patients were treated at target dose levels of 150-450 x 10^6 CAR-T cells. The primary endpoint was ORR assessed by IRC per IMWG criteria. Among 128 evaluable patients at the target dose ranges, the ORR was 73%, with a stringent CR (sCR) rate of 33%. The median time to first response was 1 month, and median DOR was 10.7 months. Median PFS was 8.8 months, and median OS was 24.8 months. MRD-negativity (10^-5 threshold) was achieved in 26% of all treated patients and 79% of patients achieving CR or better. Higher dose levels (450 x 10^6) showed improved response rates. CRS occurred in 84% (5% Grade 3+), and neurotoxicity in 18% (3% Grade 3+). Cytopenias were common and prolonged, with Grade 3+ neutropenia in 89% and Grade 3+ thrombocytopenia in 52%. No treatment-related deaths occurred. KarMMa led to FDA approval of Abecma in March 2021, the first BCMA-directed CAR-T therapy approved for myeloma. The trial established BCMA as a validated target in multiple myeloma and opened the field to multiple subsequent BCMA-targeted approaches.
outcome_summary: ORR 73%, sCR 33%. Median DOR 10.7 months, median PFS 8.8 months, median OS 24.8 months. MRD-negative 26% overall. CRS 84% (5% Grade 3+), neurotoxicity 18% (3% Grade 3+). Prolonged cytopenias common. No treatment-related deaths. First BCMA CAR-T approved (FDA March 2021).

text_summary: KarMMa-3 (NCT03651128) is a randomized, open-label, Phase 3 trial comparing idecabtagene vicleucel (Abecma, ide-cel) to standard regimens in patients with relapsed and refractory multiple myeloma (r/r MM) who had received 2-4 prior lines of therapy. This was the first randomized Phase 3 trial for a CAR-T therapy in multiple myeloma. Patients were randomized 2:1 to ide-cel (target dose 150-450 x 10^6 CAR-T cells) or one of five standard regimens chosen by the investigator (daratumumab-based, ixazomib-based, or elotuzumab-based combinations). The primary endpoint was PFS per IRC. A total of 386 patients were randomized (254 ide-cel, 132 standard regimens). The trial met its primary endpoint: median PFS was 13.3 months for ide-cel versus 4.4 months for standard regimens (HR 0.49, p<0.0001). The ORR was 71% for ide-cel versus 42% for standard therapy, and the CR/sCR rate was 39% versus 5%. The 12-month PFS rate was 55% for ide-cel versus 26% for standard regimens. At updated analysis, there was no significant OS difference, partly confounded by crossover (56% of standard-arm patients subsequently received CAR-T or other BCMA-directed therapy). CRS occurred in 88% (5% Grade 3+) and neurotoxicity in 15% (3% Grade 3+) of ide-cel-treated patients. Infections occurred in 68% of ide-cel patients, including opportunistic infections in the setting of prolonged cytopenias and immunosuppression. Movement-related neurotoxicity (parkinsonism) was observed in rare cases. The FDA approved ide-cel for the earlier-line (2-4 prior therapies) indication based on KarMMa-3 in April 2024. This trial was significant in demonstrating the superiority of CAR-T over standard therapy in a randomized setting for myeloma.
outcome_summary: Primary endpoint met: median PFS 13.3 vs 4.4 months (HR 0.49, p<0.0001). ORR 71% vs 42%, CR/sCR 39% vs 5%. 12-month PFS 55% vs 26%. CRS 88% (5% Grade 3+), neurotoxicity 15% (3% Grade 3+). OS not significantly different (crossover confounding). FDA approval for earlier-line myeloma April 2024.

text_summary: CARTITUDE-1 (NCT03548207) is a pivotal, single-arm, open-label, multi-center, Phase 1b/2 trial of ciltacabtagene autoleucel (Carvykti, cilta-cel, JNJ-4528, Janssen/Legend Biotech) in adult patients with relapsed or refractory multiple myeloma (r/r MM) who had received at least 3 prior lines of therapy including a PI, an IMiD, and an anti-CD38 antibody. Cilta-cel is a second-generation BCMA-directed CAR-T featuring a distinctive dual-epitope BCMA-binding domain (two single-domain antibodies, or VHH nanobodies, targeting two different BCMA epitopes) with a 4-1BB costimulatory domain, providing high avidity BCMA binding. The target dose was 0.75 x 10^6 CAR-T cells/kg. The primary endpoint of Phase 2 was ORR per IMWG criteria. Among 97 treated patients in the pivotal analysis, the ORR was 98% (95/97), with a stringent CR (sCR) rate of 83%. The median time to first response was 1 month. MRD-negativity (10^-5) was achieved in 92% of evaluable patients. At 27-month follow-up, median PFS was 34.9 months and median DOR was 33.9 months. The 2-year PFS rate was approximately 61%. CRS occurred in 95% (4% Grade 3+, primarily low-grade), and neurotoxicity (ICANS) in 21% (9% Grade 3+). Notably, delayed neurotoxicity including movement and neurocognitive treatment-emergent adverse events (MNTs) were observed in 12% of patients, with some cases of parkinsonism-like symptoms, cranial nerve palsies, and peripheral neuropathy occurring weeks to months post-infusion. These MNTs became a class-labeled safety concern. There were 5 Grade 5 adverse events potentially related to treatment. CARTITUDE-1 led to FDA approval of Carvykti in February 2022. The exceptionally high response rates (98% ORR, 83% sCR) established cilta-cel as the most potent CAR-T therapy in myeloma at the time of approval.
outcome_summary: ORR 98%, sCR 83%. MRD-negative 92%. Median PFS 34.9 months, median DOR 33.9 months. 2-year PFS 61%. CRS 95% (4% Grade 3+). ICANS 21% (9% Grade 3+). Delayed movement/neurocognitive toxicity in 12% (new safety signal). FDA approval February 2022.

text_summary: CARTITUDE-4 (NCT04181827) is a randomized, open-label, Phase 3 trial comparing ciltacabtagene autoleucel (Carvykti, cilta-cel) to the physician's choice of pomalidomide-bortezomib-dexamethasone (PVd) or daratumumab-pomalidomide-dexamethasone (DPd) in patients with lenalidomide-refractory multiple myeloma who had received 1-3 prior lines of therapy. This was a pivotal trial aimed at moving cilta-cel to earlier lines of myeloma treatment. Patients were randomized 1:1, with 208 patients in the cilta-cel arm and 211 in the standard therapy arm. The primary endpoint was PFS per IRC. CARTITUDE-4 met its primary endpoint at a pre-specified interim analysis: the 12-month PFS rate was 76% for cilta-cel versus 49% for standard therapy (HR 0.26, p<0.0001). The ORR was 85% for cilta-cel versus 67% for standard therapy, with CR/sCR rates of 73% versus 22%. MRD-negativity at 10^-5 was achieved in 61% of cilta-cel patients versus 16% of standard-therapy patients. At updated follow-up, median PFS was not reached for cilta-cel versus 11.8 months for standard therapy. The safety profile was consistent with prior cilta-cel studies: CRS in 76% (1% Grade 3+), ICANS in 5% (1% Grade 3+), and movement/neurocognitive treatment-emergent adverse events (MNTs) in 5%. Infections occurred in 57% of cilta-cel patients. Two T-cell malignancies were observed in the cilta-cel arm, which became a class-wide concern for CAR-T therapies. CARTITUDE-4 led to the expanded FDA approval of Carvykti for patients with 1 or more prior lines of therapy in April 2024, representing the earliest line of therapy for which any CAR-T product has been approved in myeloma.
outcome_summary: Primary endpoint met: 12-month PFS 76% vs 49% (HR 0.26, p<0.0001). ORR 85% vs 67%, CR/sCR 73% vs 22%. MRD-negative 61% vs 16%. CRS 76% (1% Grade 3+), ICANS 5% (1% Grade 3+). Two T-cell malignancies observed. FDA expanded approval for 1+ prior line April 2024.

text_summary: BELINDA (NCT03570892) is a randomized, open-label, Phase 3 trial comparing tisagenlecleucel (Kymriah, tisa-cel) to standard of care (salvage chemoimmunotherapy followed by HDT-ASCT) as second-line treatment for adult patients with aggressive B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma (DLBCL, HGBCL, TFL) that was primary refractory to or relapsed within 12 months of first-line therapy. Patients were randomized 1:1 to receive tisa-cel (n=162) or SOC (n=160). The primary endpoint was EFS per IRC. BELINDA was a negative trial: it failed to meet its primary endpoint. Median EFS was 3.0 months for tisa-cel versus 3.0 months for SOC (HR 1.07, p=0.69), showing no difference between arms. The ORR at Week 12 was 46% for tisa-cel versus 43% for SOC, and CR rates were 28% versus 31%. Multiple factors have been proposed to explain the negative result. First, extensive bridging therapy was permitted and widely used (83% of tisa-cel patients), which may have masked or diminished the CAR-T effect. Second, the decentralized manufacturing model resulted in longer vein-to-vein times (median 52 days), potentially allowing disease progression in high-risk patients. Third, the study may have included a higher proportion of patients with rapidly progressive disease who could not wait for CAR-T manufacturing. By contrast, ZUMA-7 (axi-cel, centralized manufacturing, no bridging allowed) and TRANSFORM (liso-cel, shorter manufacturing time, limited bridging) were both positive. CRS occurred in 57% (4% Grade 3+) and neurotoxicity in 11% (1% Grade 3+) in the tisa-cel arm. BELINDA's failure was a landmark event in CAR-T development, highlighting the critical importance of manufacturing turnaround time, bridging therapy strategy, and patient selection in the second-line setting.
outcome_summary: NEGATIVE TRIAL. Median EFS 3.0 vs 3.0 months (HR 1.07, p=0.69). ORR 46% vs 43%, CR 28% vs 31%. No significant difference between tisa-cel and SOC. CRS 57% (4% Grade 3+), neurotoxicity 11% (1% Grade 3+). Failure attributed to long manufacturing time, extensive bridging, and rapid disease progression.

text_summary: PILOT (NCT03483103) is an open-label, single-arm, Phase 2 trial evaluating tisagenlecleucel (Kymriah, tisa-cel) as second-line therapy in adult patients with relapsed or refractory (r/r) DLBCL who were not intended for HDT-ASCT. This study specifically targeted transplant-ineligible patients, a population often excluded from pivotal CAR-T trials. Eligible patients had r/r DLBCL after one prior line of chemoimmunotherapy and were not candidates for transplant due to age, comorbidities, or patient preference. Thirty-two patients were infused with tisa-cel at the recommended Phase 2 dose. The primary endpoint was CR rate within 6 months of infusion by IRC. While PILOT was not a randomized trial that could definitively establish superiority, the results were mixed: the CR rate was 44%, and the ORR was 56%. Median DOR was not reached for patients achieving CR. However, median PFS was 7.0 months, and median OS was 17.5 months. CRS occurred in 53% (3% Grade 3+), and neurotoxicity in 16% (6% Grade 3+). While the safety profile was manageable in this generally older, frailer population, the response rates were considered insufficient to clearly demonstrate benefit in the transplant-ineligible setting. PILOT was generally interpreted as a negative study in terms of regulatory impact, as it did not lead to label expansion for tisa-cel in the second-line transplant-ineligible setting. The trial highlighted challenges with tisa-cel manufacturing logistics and the difficulty of treating aggressive lymphoma patients who cannot tolerate the waiting period for CAR-T manufacturing. The results contrasted unfavorably with ZUMA-7 and TRANSFORM, which both met their primary endpoints.
outcome_summary: NEGATIVE/INSUFFICIENT TRIAL. CR rate 44%, ORR 56%. Median PFS 7.0 months, median OS 17.5 months. CRS 53% (3% Grade 3+), neurotoxicity 16% (6% Grade 3+). Did not lead to label expansion. Considered insufficient to demonstrate benefit in transplant-ineligible 2nd-line setting.

text_summary: ZUMA-12 (NCT03761056) is a multi-center, single-arm, open-label, Phase 2 trial of axicabtagene ciloleucel (Yescarta, axi-cel) as first-line therapy for adult patients with high-risk large B-cell lymphoma (LBCL). This was one of the earliest trials to investigate CAR-T therapy in the frontline setting. Eligible patients had high-risk LBCL defined by double/triple-hit genetics (MYC and BCL2 and/or BCL6 rearrangements, i.e., high-grade B-cell lymphoma with MYC and BCL2 or BCL6 rearrangements) or an International Prognostic Index (IPI) score of 3 or higher, with interim PET positivity (Deauville 4-5) after 2 cycles of frontline chemoimmunotherapy, indicating chemotherapy-resistant disease. Patients received standard lymphodepleting chemotherapy followed by axi-cel infusion. The primary endpoint was CR rate by independent review per Lugano criteria. Among 40 evaluable patients treated in Phase 2, the CR rate was 78% and the ORR was 89%. At a median follow-up of 15.9 months, the 12-month EFS rate was 73% and the 12-month PFS rate was 75%. The 12-month OS rate was 91%. CRS occurred in 85% (8% Grade 3+) and neurological events in 60% (25% Grade 3+), consistent with prior axi-cel experience. CAR-T cells showed robust expansion with a median peak occurring at day 8. No new safety signals were identified. ZUMA-12 provided proof-of-concept that CAR-T therapy could be effective and feasible as frontline consolidation for high-risk patients who show early chemoresistance, and it informed the design of the subsequent Phase 3 ZUMA-23 trial. The high CR rates in this traditionally poor-prognosis population (double-hit lymphoma) were particularly notable.
outcome_summary: CR 78%, ORR 89% in high-risk frontline patients. 12-month EFS 73%, PFS 75%, OS 91%. CRS 85% (8% Grade 3+), neurotoxicity 60% (25% Grade 3+). Proof-of-concept for frontline CAR-T in chemo-resistant, high-risk LBCL.

text_summary: NCT04049449 is a Phase 1b/2, open-label, multi-center study of JNJ-68284528 (later named ciltacabtagene autoleucel, cilta-cel, Carvykti) in patients with relapsed or refractory multiple myeloma. This was part of the CARTITUDE clinical development program and served as a first-in-human study outside of China (the original Chinese LEGEND-2 / LCAR-B38M study was conducted in China under a different IND). The study evaluated cilta-cel in patients who had received at least 3 prior lines of therapy including a PI, IMiD, and anti-CD38 antibody. The CAR construct features the distinctive dual-epitope BCMA-binding domain using two llama-derived VHH nanobodies (camelid single-domain antibodies) arranged in tandem, providing bivalent binding to two different epitopes on BCMA. This design confers high-avidity binding that may resist BCMA downregulation. Patients received a single infusion at a target dose of 0.75 x 10^6 CAR-T cells/kg after lymphodepleting chemotherapy with fludarabine and cyclophosphamide. Preliminary results from the Phase 1b dose-finding portion showed deep responses with an ORR exceeding 95% and high rates of MRD-negativity. The safety profile revealed CRS in most patients (predominantly low-grade) and the emergence of delayed neurotoxicity including cranial nerve palsies and movement disorders as a novel toxicity signal for BCMA-directed CAR-T therapy. The study contributed to the understanding of cilta-cel's pharmacokinetics and informed dose selection for subsequent registration studies. The Phase 2 expansion portion further characterized efficacy in specific patient subgroups. This trial was integral to the broader CARTITUDE program that ultimately led to multiple global regulatory approvals.
outcome_summary: ORR >95% in heavily pretreated myeloma. High MRD-negativity rates. CRS in most patients (mostly low-grade). Novel delayed neurotoxicity signal including cranial nerve palsies and movement disorders. Informed dose selection for CARTITUDE registration program.

text_summary: ALPHA (NCT03958656) is a Phase 1, dose-escalation trial evaluating CD22-directed chimeric antigen receptor T-cell therapy (CD22-CAR-T) in pediatric and young adult patients with relapsed or refractory (r/r) B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (B-ALL), including patients who have previously received CD19-directed therapy or have CD19-negative/dim disease. This NCI-sponsored trial addresses a critical unmet need: CD19 antigen loss or downregulation is the most common mechanism of resistance after CD19-directed CAR-T therapy, occurring in 30-60% of relapses. CD22 is an alternative B-cell lineage marker expressed on most B-ALL cells. The second-generation CAR construct uses an anti-CD22 scFv (m971) with a 4-1BB costimulatory domain and CD3-zeta activation domain. Patients aged 3-30 years with r/r B-ALL after at least one prior therapy were eligible, and notably, prior CD19-targeted immunotherapy (including CAR-T, blinatumomab) was permitted. Dose escalation evaluated 3 x 10^5 to 1 x 10^6 CD22-CAR-T cells/kg. At the recommended Phase 2 dose of 1 x 10^6 cells/kg, the CR/CRi rate was approximately 70% in heavily pretreated patients, including those with prior CD19 CAR-T failure. MRD-negativity was achieved in the majority of responders. However, the durability of responses was limited, with a median remission duration of approximately 6 months. CD22 site density and downregulation appeared to influence outcomes, with lower CD22 surface expression correlating with poorer responses. CRS occurred in the majority of patients (mostly Grade 1-2), and neurotoxicity was mild. The trial established proof-of-concept for CD22 as a viable alternative target in B-ALL and informed the development of dual CD19/CD22-targeting strategies.
outcome_summary: CR/CRi ~70% at recommended dose, including patients with prior CD19 CAR-T failure. Most responders MRD-negative. Median remission ~6 months (limited durability). CRS mostly low-grade, mild neurotoxicity. CD22 surface density influenced outcomes. Proof-of-concept for CD22 targeting after CD19 escape.

text_summary: NCT03287817 is a Phase 1, dose-escalation and dose-expansion trial of a bispecific tandem CD19/CD22-directed CAR-T cell therapy for adult patients with relapsed or refractory (r/r) B-cell malignancies, conducted at Stanford University. This innovative approach addresses the clinical challenge of antigen escape, the most common mechanism of resistance after single-antigen-targeted CAR-T therapy. The bispecific CAR construct encodes a single CAR molecule with two scFv binding domains -- one targeting CD19 and one targeting CD22 -- arranged in tandem on a single extracellular chain. This tandem design allows simultaneous engagement of both antigens on a single target cell, potentially preventing escape via loss of either antigen alone. The construct uses a 4-1BB costimulatory domain and CD3-zeta signaling domain with lentiviral transduction. Eligible patients had r/r B-ALL or B-cell NHL, including patients who had relapsed after prior CD19-directed therapy. The dose-escalation phase evaluated 1 x 10^5 to 1 x 10^6 CAR-T cells/kg. Early results demonstrated feasibility of manufacturing and clinical activity in both B-ALL and B-NHL. In the B-ALL cohort, CR rates exceeding 80% were observed, with MRD-negativity in responders. Importantly, in patients with CD19-dim or CD19-negative disease at enrollment, responses were still observed, suggesting the CD22-targeting component contributed to efficacy. CRS occurred in most patients (predominantly Grade 1-2), and neurotoxicity was manageable. Dual-antigen loss (simultaneous loss of both CD19 and CD22) was observed in rare relapsing patients, representing a new escape mechanism. This trial provided key proof-of-concept data for multi-targeted CAR-T approaches and informed larger studies of bispecific CARs.
outcome_summary: CR >80% in B-ALL cohort, including CD19-dim/negative patients. MRD-negative in responders. Feasibility of tandem bispecific CAR demonstrated. CRS mostly low-grade, manageable neurotoxicity. Rare dual-antigen loss observed. Proof-of-concept for multi-targeted CAR-T approaches.

text_summary: NCT04429008 is a Phase 1, first-in-human, dose-escalation study evaluating CD70-directed chimeric antigen receptor T-cell therapy in adult patients with relapsed or refractory (r/r) acute myeloid leukemia (AML). CD70 is a TNF superfamily member selectively expressed on AML blasts and leukemic stem cells while having limited expression on normal hematopoietic stem cells, making it an attractive target for CAR-T therapy in AML where suitable surface antigens have been difficult to identify. The CAR construct uses an anti-CD70 scFv derived from a fully human antibody, with a 4-1BB costimulatory domain and CD3-zeta activation domain. To mitigate fratricide (CD70 is transiently expressed on activated T cells), the construct incorporates strategies to prevent T-cell self-killing during manufacturing and expansion. Eligible patients had r/r AML after at least one prior line of therapy, excluding acute promyelocytic leukemia. The study evaluated multiple dose levels to establish safety and the recommended Phase 2 dose. Preliminary results demonstrated that CD70 CAR-T cells could be successfully manufactured and infused with manageable toxicity. CRS was observed but generally low-grade. Anti-leukemic activity was observed with blast reductions in bone marrow and peripheral blood, though durable complete remissions were limited, reflecting the broader challenge of CAR-T therapy in AML: the immunosuppressive myeloid microenvironment, T-cell exhaustion, and on-target off-tumor effects on myeloid precursors leading to prolonged myelosuppression. The trial provided important data on CD70 as a target antigen in AML and the feasibility of CAR-T approaches in myeloid malignancies, an area of intense investigation.
outcome_summary: Feasibility and safety demonstrated. CRS generally low-grade. Anti-leukemic activity observed with blast reductions, but durable CRs limited. Prolonged myelosuppression as on-target off-tumor effect. CD70 validated as targetable antigen in AML. Challenges of myeloid microenvironment highlighted.

text_summary: NCT04649073 is a Phase 1/2, single-center study evaluating MCARH109, an FcRH5 (Fc receptor-homolog 5, also known as FCRL5)-directed chimeric antigen receptor T-cell therapy, in patients with relapsed or refractory (r/r) multiple myeloma, conducted at Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center (MSKCC). FcRH5 is a novel target antigen expressed on malignant plasma cells with more restricted normal tissue expression compared to BCMA, potentially offering a differentiated safety profile and an alternative for patients who relapse after BCMA-directed therapies. The CAR construct utilizes a fully human anti-FcRH5 scFv with a 4-1BB costimulatory domain and CD3-zeta signaling domain. Patients with r/r MM who had received at least 3 prior lines of therapy, including a PI, IMiD, and anti-CD38 antibody, were eligible. Notably, patients with prior BCMA-directed therapy (including BCMA CAR-T and bispecific antibodies) were also eligible, addressing the growing population of BCMA-exposed/refractory patients. Dose escalation evaluated multiple dose levels. Preliminary results presented at ASH 2022 and 2023 showed encouraging clinical activity: the ORR was approximately 60% across dose levels, including responses in patients who had previously received BCMA-targeted therapy. CRS occurred in the majority of patients but was predominantly low-grade (Grade 1-2). Neurotoxicity was mild and infrequent. Cytopenias were common, consistent with lymphodepleting chemotherapy and the myeloma disease burden. The trial established FcRH5 as a viable and active target in myeloma, distinct from BCMA and GPRC5D, and provided important data for developing next-generation myeloma CAR-T therapies that could be sequenced after BCMA-directed approaches.
outcome_summary: ORR ~60% across dose levels, including responses in prior BCMA-treated patients. CRS mostly low-grade. Mild, infrequent neurotoxicity. FcRH5 validated as viable myeloma target distinct from BCMA. Active in BCMA-exposed/refractory patients, important for sequencing strategies.

text_summary: NCT03399448 is a Phase 1, first-in-human, dose-escalation study of GPRC5D-directed chimeric antigen receptor T-cell therapy in patients with relapsed or refractory (r/r) multiple myeloma. GPRC5D (G-protein-coupled receptor class C group 5 member D) is an orphan receptor highly expressed on malignant plasma cells and hair follicles, with minimal expression on other normal tissues. As a non-BCMA target, GPRC5D-directed CAR-T therapy offers a potential treatment option for patients who progress after BCMA-targeted therapies. The CAR construct uses a GPRC5D-targeting binding domain with a 4-1BB costimulatory domain and CD3-zeta signaling domain. Patients with r/r MM who had received at least 3 prior lines of therapy were eligible, including patients with prior BCMA-directed therapy exposure. The study evaluated multiple dose levels to determine safety, tolerability, and preliminary efficacy. Results presented at major hematology conferences demonstrated anti-myeloma activity with an ORR of approximately 70% at higher dose levels, including stringent complete responses. Notably, responses were observed irrespective of prior BCMA-directed therapy, confirming the independent target. CRS occurred in most patients (predominantly Grade 1-2), and a distinctive on-target, off-tumor adverse effect of skin-related toxicity (nail changes, skin rash, and dysgeusia/taste changes) was observed, reflecting GPRC5D expression on skin appendages. These dermatologic effects were manageable and generally self-limited. Neurotoxicity was infrequent and generally mild. The trial provided critical proof-of-concept for GPRC5D as an alternative myeloma target and informed the development of multiple GPRC5D-directed therapies, including bispecific antibodies such as talquetamab (approved) and other CAR-T constructs.
outcome_summary: ORR ~70% at higher dose levels, including sCR. Responses irrespective of prior BCMA therapy. CRS mostly low-grade. Distinctive on-target skin toxicity (nail changes, dysgeusia, rash). Mild neurotoxicity. GPRC5D validated as BCMA-independent myeloma target. Informed development of talquetamab and other GPRC5D-directed therapies.

text_summary: NCT04097301 is a Phase 1, first-in-human study of CB-010, a CRISPR-edited, allogeneic (off-the-shelf) anti-CD19 CAR-T cell therapy developed by Caribou Biosciences (formerly also associated with CTX110 nomenclature from CRISPR Therapeutics, though CB-010 is the Caribou product -- the original study NCT04035434 for CTX110 by CRISPR Therapeutics was a separate but related allogeneic program). CB-010 utilizes CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing to knock out the endogenous T-cell receptor (TCR) alpha chain (TRAC locus) to prevent graft-versus-host disease (GvHD) and to knock out PD-1 (PDCD1 gene) to enhance anti-tumor activity by eliminating this inhibitory checkpoint. The CAR construct is inserted into the TRAC locus via adeno-associated virus (AAV)-mediated homology-directed repair, providing more controlled CAR expression under the endogenous TCR promoter. This allogeneic approach enables manufacturing from healthy donor T cells, potentially eliminating vein-to-vein time delays and providing an immediately available product. The study enrolled patients with r/r B-cell NHL, including DLBCL, who had failed at least 2 prior lines of therapy. Dose escalation evaluated multiple dose levels. Early results from the ANTLER Phase 1 study showed an ORR of 78% at higher dose levels, with a CR rate of 44%. CRS was generally low-grade. GvHD, the major concern with allogeneic CAR-T products, was not observed, validating the TRAC knockout strategy. However, the durability of responses was limited compared to autologous products, with most patients eventually progressing, likely due to host-mediated rejection of the allogeneic cells. The PD-1 knockout did not appear to produce unexpected immune-related adverse events. The trial was a significant advance for the allogeneic CAR-T field.
outcome_summary: ORR 78% at higher dose levels, CR 44%. CRS generally low-grade. No GvHD observed (TRAC knockout effective). Limited durability due to likely host rejection of allogeneic cells. PD-1 knockout did not cause unexpected adverse events. Important proof-of-concept for CRISPR-edited allogeneic CAR-T.

text_summary: NCT01029366 is the landmark, first-in-human, single-center, pilot study of CD19-directed chimeric antigen receptor T-cell therapy conducted at the University of Pennsylvania by Carl June and colleagues. This trial treated the first patients to receive CAR-T therapy using a lentiviral-transduced, second-generation CAR construct with a 4-1BB costimulatory domain and CD3-zeta signaling domain targeting CD19, which later became tisagenlecleucel (Kymriah). The study enrolled adults with relapsed or refractory CD19-positive malignancies, initially focusing on chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL) and subsequently B-ALL. The first three CLL patients were treated in 2010, including the widely reported cases of patients achieving durable complete remissions. Patient UPN01 (Doug Olson) and patient UPN03 (Bill Ludwig, deceased 2021 from unrelated causes) achieved complete molecular remissions in CLL that persisted for over a decade, demonstrating the curative potential of CAR-T therapy. The B-ALL cohort included the historic case of Emily Whitehead (treated in 2012 at age 6), who was the first pediatric patient to receive CAR-T therapy and remains in sustained remission over a decade later. Her severe CRS, managed with tocilizumab (repurposed from rheumatology), established the CRS management paradigm now used globally. The overall CR rate in the B-ALL cohort was 90%, with MRD-negativity in responders. In the CLL cohort, overall response rates were more modest (~40-50%), but responses in complete responders were remarkably durable. This trial generated the clinical data, manufacturing expertise, and safety management protocols that underpinned all subsequent CAR-T development. It demonstrated that CAR-T cells could persist for years, undergo in vivo expansion greater than 1000-fold, and establish immunological memory.
outcome_summary: Historic first-in-human CAR-T trial. CLL: ~40-50% ORR, but extraordinary 10+ year durable CRs in subset. B-ALL: ~90% CR. Included Emily Whitehead (first pediatric patient, sustained remission 10+ years). Established tocilizumab for CRS management. Demonstrated CAR-T persistence >10 years. Foundational for entire field.

text_summary: NCT04503980 is a Phase 1, first-in-human, dose-escalation study of FT819, an iPSC-derived, allogeneic anti-CD19 CAR-T cell therapy developed by Fate Therapeutics. FT819 represents a fundamentally different manufacturing paradigm for CAR-T therapy: rather than starting from donor peripheral blood T cells, it is derived from a clonal, master-engineered induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC) line that has been gene-edited to incorporate the CAR transgene and multiple functional enhancements. The iPSC line is differentiated into T cells in vitro, producing a homogeneous, standardized cell product. Key genetic modifications include: insertion of the anti-CD19 CAR into the TRAC locus (eliminating TCR expression to prevent GvHD), expression of a novel 1XX signaling domain (a modified CD3-zeta with a single functional ITAM to reduce exhaustion), and other edits to enhance persistence and resist host rejection. This approach enables potentially unlimited manufacturing scale from a single iPSC clone, addressing the scalability limitations of both autologous and donor-derived allogeneic approaches. The study enrolled adult patients with r/r B-cell lymphoma and CLL who had received at least 2 prior lines of therapy. Dose escalation evaluated multiple dose levels. Preliminary data demonstrated feasibility of the iPSC-to-T-cell manufacturing process, successful engraftment and expansion of the iPSC-derived CAR-T cells in patients, and initial anti-tumor activity with tumor reductions observed. CRS was generally low-grade, and no GvHD was observed. The persistence of iPSC-derived CAR-T cells appeared limited, with most cells becoming undetectable by 28 days, reflecting the ongoing challenge of host immune rejection in the allogeneic setting. Fate Therapeutics subsequently discontinued FT819 development to focus on next-generation iPSC-derived products with enhanced anti-rejection modifications.
outcome_summary: Feasibility of iPSC-derived CAR-T demonstrated. Engraftment and initial anti-tumor activity observed. CRS low-grade, no GvHD. Limited persistence (~28 days) due to host rejection. Development discontinued in favor of next-generation iPSC products. Important proof-of-concept for iPSC-to-T-cell manufacturing paradigm.

text_summary: NCT02159716 is a Phase 1, dose-escalation study evaluating mesothelin-directed chimeric antigen receptor T cells engineered to co-express a dominant negative PD-1 receptor (dnPD-1), administered to patients with recurrent or metastatic mesothelin-expressing solid tumors, conducted at the University of Pennsylvania. This trial was one of the earliest to combine CAR-T therapy with checkpoint modification for solid tumors. Mesothelin is a glycoprotein highly expressed on the surface of malignant pleural mesothelioma, pancreatic adenocarcinoma, and ovarian cancer cells, with limited normal tissue expression (primarily mesothelial lining of pleura, peritoneum, and pericardium). The second-generation CAR construct uses an anti-mesothelin SS1 scFv with a 4-1BB costimulatory domain and CD3-zeta signaling domain. The dominant negative PD-1 receptor lacks the intracellular signaling domain, acting as a decoy that competes with endogenous PD-1 for PD-L1 binding without transmitting inhibitory signals, thus locally overcoming the immunosuppressive PD-L1-rich tumor microenvironment. Patients with advanced mesothelioma, pancreatic cancer, or ovarian cancer were enrolled. The study evaluated intravenous administration of CAR-T cells with and without cyclophosphamide preconditioning. Results demonstrated that mesothelin CAR-T cells could traffic to tumor sites (confirmed by tumor biopsies showing CAR-T infiltration) and showed evidence of anti-tumor activity in some patients, including metabolic responses on PET imaging and tumor marker reductions. However, durable objective responses by RECIST criteria were rare. CRS was minimal, reflecting limited CAR-T expansion in the solid tumor setting. No significant on-target off-tumor toxicity against normal mesothelial surfaces was observed. The trial provided critical insights into the barriers to CAR-T efficacy in solid tumors, including the immunosuppressive microenvironment, antigen heterogeneity, and limited T-cell infiltration and persistence.
outcome_summary: CAR-T trafficking to tumors confirmed by biopsy. Metabolic responses and tumor marker reductions in some patients. Durable RECIST responses rare. Minimal CRS. No significant on-target off-tumor toxicity against mesothelial surfaces. Demonstrated barriers of solid tumor microenvironment. Important early solid tumor CAR-T trial with PD-1 modification.

text_summary: NCT04489862 is a Phase 1, dose-escalation and dose-expansion study of GPC3 (Glypican-3)-directed chimeric antigen receptor T-cell therapy for patients with advanced hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC). GPC3 is a heparan sulfate proteoglycan that is highly expressed on the surface of HCC cells (present in approximately 70-80% of HCC tumors) while being absent from normal adult hepatocytes, making it one of the most promising tumor-associated antigens for CAR-T therapy in liver cancer. The CAR construct utilizes an anti-GPC3 scFv with 4-1BB costimulatory and CD3-zeta signaling domains, with humanized binding domains to reduce immunogenicity. Eligible patients had advanced, unresectable HCC that had progressed on or was intolerant to standard systemic therapy (sorafenib, lenvatinib, or checkpoint inhibitors). The study evaluated both intravenous and hepatic artery infusion routes of CAR-T cell delivery, the latter intended to increase local concentration of CAR-T cells within the liver tumor microenvironment. Dose escalation established the safety and maximum tolerated dose. Preliminary results showed that GPC3 CAR-T cells could be safely administered with manageable CRS (mostly Grade 1-2). Evidence of anti-tumor activity was observed, including partial responses by RECIST and mRECIST criteria, tumor marker (AFP) reductions, and tumor necrosis on imaging. Hepatic artery infusion appeared to enhance tumor infiltration compared to intravenous delivery. However, the dense fibrotic stroma, immunosuppressive microenvironment (regulatory T cells, myeloid-derived suppressor cells, PD-L1 expression), and antigenic heterogeneity of HCC limited the depth and durability of responses. Liver-specific toxicities including transient transaminase elevations were observed but manageable. The trial contributed important data on locoregional CAR-T delivery strategies for solid tumors.
outcome_summary: Manageable CRS (mostly Grade 1-2). Partial responses, AFP reductions, and tumor necrosis observed. Hepatic artery infusion enhanced tumor infiltration vs IV. Response durability limited by immunosuppressive microenvironment. Transient transaminase elevations manageable. Important data for locoregional CAR-T delivery in solid tumors.

text_summary: NCT02107963 is a Phase 1/2, dose-escalation study evaluating GD2-directed chimeric antigen receptor T-cell therapy (GD2 CAR-T) in children and young adults with relapsed or refractory neuroblastoma, conducted at Baylor College of Medicine. GD2 (disialoganglioside) is a glycolipid abundantly expressed on neuroblastoma cells and has been a validated immunotherapy target since the approval of the anti-GD2 monoclonal antibody dinutuximab. The third-generation CAR construct incorporates an anti-GD2 scFv (14g2a-derived) with both CD28 and OX40 (CD134) costimulatory domains plus a CD3-zeta signaling domain, designed to enhance T-cell persistence and anti-tumor activity through dual costimulation. Eligible patients had relapsed or refractory high-risk neuroblastoma after standard multimodal therapy. Patients received lymphodepleting chemotherapy followed by GD2 CAR-T infusion. The third-generation dual-costimulatory design was chosen based on preclinical data suggesting improved in vivo persistence compared to second-generation constructs in the solid tumor setting. Results showed that GD2 CAR-T cells could be safely manufactured and administered to pediatric patients. Objective responses were observed, including complete responses in patients with bone and bone marrow disease. Response rates were approximately 30-40% overall, with higher activity against bone marrow disease compared to bulky soft tissue tumors. GD2 CAR-T cells showed in vivo expansion and persistence, with detectable CAR-T cells for several weeks to months. CRS was generally mild to moderate. A notable safety consideration was pain at tumor sites (tumor-associated inflammation), consistent with GD2-targeting. Neurotoxicity was mild. The trial demonstrated the potential of CAR-T therapy for a pediatric solid tumor and informed subsequent studies combining GD2 CAR-T with checkpoint inhibitors or other immune-modulating strategies.
outcome_summary: ORR ~30-40%, CRs in bone/bone marrow disease. Higher activity in marrow vs soft tissue tumors. Mild-moderate CRS. Tumor site pain as expected on-target effect. GD2 CAR-T persistence for weeks to months. Third-generation (CD28+OX40) design. Important proof-of-concept for CAR-T in pediatric solid tumors.

text_summary: NCT04556266 is a Phase 1, first-in-human, dose-escalation study evaluating TROP2 (trophoblast cell-surface antigen 2)-directed chimeric antigen receptor T-cell therapy in patients with advanced, treatment-refractory solid tumors expressing TROP2. TROP2 is a transmembrane glycoprotein overexpressed in a wide variety of epithelial cancers including triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC), non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), urothelial carcinoma, and colorectal cancer. TROP2 has been validated as a therapeutic target by the success of the antibody-drug conjugate sacituzumab govitecan (Trodelvy). The CAR-T approach aims to harness the cytotoxic power of engineered T cells against TROP2-expressing tumors. The second-generation CAR construct uses an anti-TROP2 binding domain with a 4-1BB costimulatory domain, designed to minimize recognition of normal epithelial tissues expressing low levels of TROP2 through affinity tuning of the scFv. Eligible patients had advanced solid tumors with confirmed TROP2 expression (by immunohistochemistry) that had progressed on standard therapies. The study evaluated intravenous infusion of TROP2 CAR-T cells at escalating doses following lymphodepleting chemotherapy. Preliminary results demonstrated feasibility of manufacturing and safety at initial dose levels. CRS was observed but generally manageable. On-target off-tumor toxicity against normal TROP2-expressing epithelium (skin, gastrointestinal mucosa) was a key monitored safety concern, with mild to moderate mucosal inflammation observed in some patients. Anti-tumor activity signals included stable disease and minor tumor regressions, though confirmed partial responses were limited at early dose levels. The trial highlighted the importance of scFv affinity optimization to achieve a therapeutic window between tumor and normal tissue TROP2 expression levels.
outcome_summary: Feasibility and safety demonstrated at initial dose levels. Manageable CRS. Mild-moderate mucosal inflammation as on-target off-tumor effect. Stable disease and minor regressions; confirmed PRs limited at early doses. Affinity tuning critical for therapeutic window. Important exploration of validated ADC target via CAR-T approach.

text_summary: NCT04430530 is a Phase 1/2, multi-center, dose-escalation and dose-expansion study of CLL-1 (C-type lectin-like molecule-1, also known as CLEC12A)-directed chimeric antigen receptor T-cell therapy in patients with relapsed or refractory (r/r) acute myeloid leukemia (AML). CLL-1 is a transmembrane glycoprotein selectively expressed on AML blasts and leukemic stem cells (LSCs) in approximately 90% of AML cases. Critically, CLL-1 is absent on normal hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs), providing a potential therapeutic window that differentiates it from other myeloid targets such as CD33 or CD123, which are also expressed on normal HSCs. The second-generation CAR construct uses an anti-CLL-1 scFv with a 4-1BB costimulatory domain and CD3-zeta activation domain. Eligible patients had r/r AML after at least one prior line of therapy, including patients who relapsed after allogeneic stem cell transplant. The Phase 1 portion established safety and the recommended Phase 2 dose through dose escalation. Preliminary results demonstrated feasibility of CLL-1 CAR-T manufacturing and clinical activity with blast reductions in bone marrow and achievement of morphologic complete remissions in a subset of patients. CRS occurred in most patients (predominantly Grade 1-2, with rare Grade 3 events). Importantly, normal hematopoietic recovery was observed in responding patients, supporting the hypothesis that CLL-1 is not expressed on normal HSCs and that CLL-1 CAR-T therapy may spare normal hematopoiesis -- a significant advantage over CD33/CD123-directed approaches. Cytopenias were observed but were attributable to lymphodepleting chemotherapy and disease. Some patients were able to proceed to allogeneic transplant consolidation in remission. The trial represents an important advance in developing CAR-T therapy for AML, a disease where target antigen selection has been a major challenge.
outcome_summary: Blast reductions and morphologic CRs in subset of patients. CRS mostly Grade 1-2. Normal hematopoietic recovery observed, supporting CLL-1 sparing of normal HSCs. Some patients bridged to allogeneic transplant. CLL-1 validated as promising AML target with potential therapeutic window over CD33/CD123.

text_summary: ICAR-1 (NCT04816526) is a Phase 1, first-in-human study evaluating a novel CAR-T cell product incorporating an inducible caspase-9 (iCasp9) safety switch in patients with relapsed or refractory (r/r) B-cell malignancies. The iCasp9 system is an engineered suicide gene that enables rapid, selective elimination of CAR-T cells upon administration of a small-molecule chemical inducer of dimerization (CID, AP1903/rimiducid). When the CID is administered, the iCasp9 molecules dimerize and activate the caspase cascade, inducing apoptosis of the CAR-T cells within hours. This safety mechanism is designed to address life-threatening CRS, ICANS, or other severe CAR-T-related toxicities that are refractory to standard management with tocilizumab and corticosteroids. The CAR construct is second-generation with an anti-CD19 scFv, 4-1BB costimulatory domain, CD3-zeta signaling domain, and co-expression of the iCasp9 gene via a 2A peptide linkage. Eligible patients had r/r CD19-positive B-cell NHL or B-ALL. The primary endpoints included safety, tolerability, and characterization of the iCasp9 safety switch pharmacodynamics. Preliminary results demonstrated that iCasp9 CAR-T cells showed comparable anti-tumor efficacy to conventional CD19 CAR-T cells, with ORRs consistent with the existing CD19 CAR-T literature. Importantly, in patients who developed severe toxicity and received CID, rapid elimination of circulating CAR-T cells (>90% depletion within 2 hours) was observed, with concurrent resolution of CRS symptoms. The safety switch was activated in a small number of patients with Grade 3+ CRS, and all showed rapid clinical improvement. The trial established proof-of-concept for pharmacologically controllable CAR-T safety switches and informed the development of next-generation switchable CAR-T platforms.
outcome_summary: Anti-tumor efficacy comparable to conventional CD19 CARs. iCasp9 safety switch successfully activated: >90% CAR-T depletion within 2 hours of CID administration, with rapid CRS resolution. Proof-of-concept for pharmacologically controllable safety switches in CAR-T therapy. Informs next-generation switchable CAR-T platforms.

text_summary: NCT04693676 is a Phase 1, dose-escalation and dose-expansion study of EpCAM (epithelial cell adhesion molecule)-directed chimeric antigen receptor T-cell therapy administered via intraperitoneal infusion for patients with EpCAM-positive peritoneal carcinomatosis from gastrointestinal or ovarian cancers. EpCAM is a transmembrane glycoprotein widely expressed on epithelial tumors, particularly colorectal, gastric, pancreatic, and ovarian cancers. While EpCAM is also expressed on normal epithelial tissues, the intraperitoneal delivery route is designed to achieve high local CAR-T concentrations within the peritoneal cavity while limiting systemic exposure and on-target off-tumor toxicity against normal EpCAM-expressing tissues in other organs. The second-generation CAR construct uses a humanized anti-EpCAM scFv with a 4-1BB costimulatory domain and CD3-zeta signaling domain. Eligible patients had recurrent peritoneal carcinomatosis from colorectal cancer, gastric cancer, pancreatic cancer, or ovarian cancer that had progressed on standard systemic therapies, with EpCAM expression confirmed by immunohistochemistry. The intraperitoneal infusion was performed via an implanted peritoneal catheter or at the time of surgical placement. Dose escalation evaluated escalating CAR-T cell doses. Preliminary results demonstrated feasibility of intraperitoneal CAR-T delivery with manageable toxicity. Localized peritoneal inflammation (chemical peritonitis) was the most common adverse event, reflecting local CAR-T activation. Systemic CRS was milder than typically seen with intravenous CAR-T administration. Ascites reduction was observed in multiple patients, and some patients showed radiographic reductions in peritoneal tumor burden. Cytology conversion from positive to negative was seen in a subset of patients. However, responses in bulky solid peritoneal deposits were limited. The trial provided important proof-of-concept for locoregional CAR-T delivery strategies in peritoneal disease.
outcome_summary: Intraperitoneal delivery feasible with manageable toxicity. Localized peritoneal inflammation common; systemic CRS milder than IV delivery. Ascites reduction and peritoneal tumor burden reductions observed. Cytology conversion in subset. Limited activity in bulky solid deposits. Proof-of-concept for locoregional CAR-T delivery in peritoneal disease.

title: CARTITUDE-4: Ciltacabtagene Autoleucel vs Standard of Care in Lenalidomide-Refractory Multiple Myeloma
text_summary: CARTITUDE-4 is a landmark Phase III randomized trial comparing cilta-cel to physician's choice (PVd or DPd) in patients with lenalidomide-refractory multiple myeloma after 1-3 prior lines. This is the first randomized trial demonstrating CAR-T superiority over active comparator in myeloma. Primary endpoint: PFS (HR 0.26, p<0.0001). Median PFS not reached for cilta-cel vs 11.8 months SOC. ORR 84.6% vs 67.3%, CR 73.1% vs 21.8%, MRD negativity (10^-5) 60.6% vs 15.6%. CRS 76.1% (grade ≥3: 1.1%), ICANS 4.5%. Delayed neurotoxicity (movement/cognitive): 5.0%. Enrollment: 419 patients across 81 sites in 22 countries. Median follow-up 28 months. This trial fundamentally shifts the myeloma treatment paradigm by moving CAR-T to earlier lines. FDA supplemental approval for 1-3 prior lines granted based on these results.

title: KarMMa-3: Idecabtagene Vicleucel vs Standard Regimens in Relapsed/Refractory Multiple Myeloma
text_summary: KarMMa-3 randomized 386 patients with RRMM after 2-4 prior lines (including PI, IMiD, and anti-CD38) to ide-cel versus standard regimens (DPd, DVd, IRd, Kd, or EPd). Ide-cel demonstrated significant PFS benefit (median 13.3 vs 4.4 months, HR 0.49, p<0.001). ORR was 71% vs 42%, with ≥CR rate of 39% vs 5%. CRS occurred in 88% (grade ≥3: 5%), ICANS in 15% (grade ≥3: 3%). This was the first randomized trial for ide-cel and confirmed its superiority over active comparator, supporting its use in 2-4 prior lines rather than only after ≥4 lines.
outcome_summary: PFS 13.3 vs 4.4 months (HR 0.49), ORR 71% vs 42%

title: ZUMA-7: Axicabtagene Ciloleucel vs Standard of Care in Second-Line Large B-Cell Lymphoma — 4-Year Follow-Up
text_summary: ZUMA-7 randomized 359 patients with relapsed/refractory LBCL within 12 months of first-line therapy to axi-cel vs SOC (salvage chemotherapy + auto-HSCT). At 4-year follow-up, axi-cel showed significant OS benefit (median 46.2 vs 24.5 months, HR 0.73, p=0.027). Four-year EFS was 41.8% vs 24.4%. Late relapses beyond 24 months were rare (3%). Grade ≥3 CRS 6%, ICANS 21%. This established axi-cel as standard of care for 2L LBCL with durable long-term benefit.

title: TRANSFORM: Lisocabtagene Maraleucel vs Standard of Care in Second-Line Large B-Cell Lymphoma
text_summary: TRANSFORM randomized 184 patients with relapsed/refractory LBCL within 12 months of first-line therapy to liso-cel vs SOC (salvage chemo + auto-HSCT). Liso-cel demonstrated superior EFS (median 10.1 vs 2.3 months, HR 0.35, p<0.001). CR rate was 74% vs 43%. Defined CD4+/CD8+ composition showed consistent manufacturing and lower neurotoxicity (ICANS grade ≥3: 4% vs conventional). CRS grade ≥3 was 1%. OS benefit observed at 2-year follow-up.
outcome_summary: EFS 10.1 vs 2.3 months (HR 0.35), CR 74% vs 43%

title: CARBON: CTX110 Allogeneic CRISPR-Edited Anti-CD19 CAR-T in B-Cell Malignancies
text_summary: CTX110 is an allogeneic anti-CD19 CAR-T with CRISPR-mediated TRAC and B2M knockout from healthy donor T cells. Phase I/II enrolled 58 patients with R/R LBCL. At recommended Phase II dose, ORR 58%, CR 37%. Median DOR 10.2 months. No GvHD observed (0%), validating TRAC knockout. CRS 56% (grade ≥3: 5%), ICANS 12%. Host anti-product immune responses detected in 35%. Off-the-shelf availability within 2 days vs 3-6 weeks for autologous. Demonstrates feasibility of gene-edited allogeneic CAR-T.
outcome_summary: ORR 58%, CR 37%, 0% GvHD, off-the-shelf in 2 days

title: ALLO-501A: Allogeneic Anti-CD19 CAR-T with Anti-CD52 Lymphodepletion in R/R LBCL
text_summary: ALLO-501A uses allogeneic donor T cells with TALEN-mediated TCR/CD52 knockout, enabling use of anti-CD52 (ALLO-647) for enhanced lymphodepletion without eliminating the product. Phase I enrolled 43 patients with R/R LBCL. ORR 67%, CR 44% at 3 months. CRS 50% (grade ≥3: 3%), no GvHD. Enhanced lymphodepletion with anti-CD52 improved CAR-T expansion 3-fold vs standard Flu/Cy alone. Median product persistence 28 days. Re-dosing feasible in 8 patients (additional responses in 3).
outcome_summary: ORR 67%, CR 44%, 0% GvHD, re-dosing feasible

title: FT819: First-in-Human iPSC-Derived Anti-CD19 CAR-T in B-Cell Malignancies
text_summary: FT819 is the first iPSC-derived CAR-T to enter clinical testing. Manufactured from clonal master iPSC line with CAR inserted at TRAC locus via CRISPR. Phase I enrolled 19 patients with R/R B-cell malignancies. At highest dose (900M cells): ORR 47%, CR 26%. Peak expansion 5-10x lower than autologous. Median persistence 21 days. CRS mild (grade 1-2 only, 58%). No GvHD, no teratoma/tumorigenicity. Manufacturing from unlimited iPSC source enables thousands of doses per cell line.
outcome_summary: ORR 47%, CR 26%, no GvHD, no teratoma, iPSC-derived

title: Anti-CD19 CAR-T for Refractory Systemic Lupus Erythematosus: Phase II Multi-Center Trial
text_summary: Expanding on the landmark Mackensen et al. results, this Phase II trial enrolled 30 patients with refractory SLE (SLEDAI >10 despite ≥3 immunosuppressants). All 30 achieved drug-free remission. Anti-dsDNA antibodies undetectable by month 3 in 93%. Complement normalized in 87%. Proteinuria resolved in 100% of lupus nephritis. B-cell recovery at months 3-6 with naive phenotype; pathogenic autoantibodies did not return. CRS grade 1-2 in 80%, no grade ≥3. No ICANS. Functional cure paradigm for severe autoimmune disease.
outcome_summary: 100% drug-free remission, anti-dsDNA undetectable, no grade ≥3 CRS

title: GPRC5D-Targeted CAR-T in Relapsed/Refractory Multiple Myeloma Including BCMA-Refractory Patients
text_summary: Phase I dose-escalation of GPRC5D-directed CAR-T using nanobody-based binder at MSKCC. 32 patients enrolled including 15 who had failed prior BCMA-targeted therapy. ORR 71% overall, 60% in BCMA-refractory cohort. CR 44%. CRS 81% (grade ≥3: 6%). GPRC5D-specific toxicities: nail changes 47%, dysgeusia 44%, skin dryness 34% — all reversible. No ICANS. Demonstrates viability of non-BCMA myeloma target with distinct toxicity profile. Dual BCMA/GPRC5D sequential targeting strategy enabled.
outcome_summary: ORR 71%, 60% in BCMA-refractory, nail/skin toxicity reversible

title: Bispecific CD19/CD22 CAR-T in Relapsed B-ALL After Prior CD19 CAR-T Failure
text_summary: Multicenter Phase I/II evaluating tandem bispecific CD19/CD22 CAR-T (FMC63 + m971) in patients who relapsed after prior CD19 CAR-T with CD19 loss or dim expression. 45 patients enrolled. ORR 78%, CR/CRi 71%. In CD19-negative patients: CR 65%, confirming CD22-mediated killing. MRD negativity (10^-4) in 60% of responders. Median EFS 12.4 months. CRS 82% (grade ≥3: 11%). Only 8% showed dual antigen escape. Establishes bispecific as rescue strategy for CD19 CAR-T failures.
outcome_summary: ORR 78%, CR 71% (65% in CD19-neg), 8% dual escape

title: CT041: Claudin18.2-Targeted CAR-T in Advanced Gastric and Pancreatic Adenocarcinoma
text_summary: Pivotal Phase II of CT041 (CARsgen) in Claudin18.2+ (≥70%, ≥2+ intensity) advanced GI cancers. 71 patients: 48 gastric/GEJ, 23 pancreatic. Gastric ORR 57.4%, median DOR 8.4 months, median OS 18.3 months. Pancreatic ORR 30.4%, median OS 12.1 months (vs 6 months historical). CRS 83% (grade ≥3: 4.2%). GI toxicity 38% (on-target). Claudin18.2 maintained in 85% of post-treatment biopsies. First CAR-T showing meaningful solid tumor GI cancer efficacy.
outcome_summary: Gastric ORR 57.4%, Pancreatic ORR 30.4%, CLDN18.2 retained 85%

title: CYAD-101: Allogeneic NKG2D-Based CAR-T Combined with FOLFOX in MSS Metastatic Colorectal Cancer
text_summary: CYAD-101 is allogeneic NKG2D CAR-T using TIM peptide (non-gene-edited GvHD prevention) combined with FOLFOX chemotherapy. Phase Ib: 36 patients with MSS mCRC. DCR 72%, ORR 8.3%. Median PFS 5.9 months (vs 4.5 historical). No GvHD. CRS mild (grade 1-2 in 44%). Chemotherapy upregulated NKG2D ligands on tumor cells, demonstrating synergistic combination mechanism. First allogeneic CAR-T in colorectal cancer.
outcome_summary: DCR 72%, ORR 8.3%, synergy with FOLFOX, no GvHD

title: GD2-Targeted CAR-T with C7R Armoring for Relapsed/Refractory Neuroblastoma
text_summary: Phase I at Baylor evaluating GD2-CAR-T with constitutive IL-7 receptor (C7R) providing tonic survival signaling. 15 patients with relapsed neuroblastoma after anti-GD2 antibody. ORR 40%, including 2 CR in bone marrow disease. C7R armoring extended CAR-T persistence (median 6 months vs 3 weeks for non-armored GD2 CAR-T historical). Neuropathic pain in 40% (managed with gabapentin). CRS 47% (grade ≥3: 7%). Demonstrates armoring overcomes solid tumor persistence limitation.
outcome_summary: ORR 40%, persistence 6 months (vs 3 weeks historical), C7R armored

title: GD2-CAR-T Administered Intraventricularly for Diffuse Midline Glioma (DIPG/DMG)
text_summary: Phase I at Stanford evaluating intracerebroventricular (ICV) delivery of GD2-CAR-T in H3K27M-mutant DMG. 12 patients age 4-23 years. Repeated ICV dosing (up to 4 infusions). Radiographic response in 40% (4/10 evaluable: 1 CR, 3 PR by RANO). 3 patients sustained response >6 months. No systemic CRS. CNS inflammation universal (headache, fever). CSF cytokine analysis confirmed robust local CAR-T activation. Proof-of-concept for regional CNS CAR-T delivery in universally fatal pediatric tumor.
outcome_summary: 40% radiographic response, ICV delivery, no systemic CRS

title: B7-H3-Targeted CAR-T Administered Intracranially for Recurrent Pediatric CNS Tumors
text_summary: Phase I at Seattle Children's: ICV delivery of B7-H3-CAR-T in 18 patients with recurrent CNS tumors (8 DIPG/DMG, 5 ependymoma, 5 medulloblastoma). Repeated dosing (up to 4 infusions). Radiographic response in 33% (6/18: 2 CR, 4 PR). All responses with ≥2 infusions. High CSF CAR-T concentration (100-1000x peripheral). CNS inflammation manageable. No systemic CRS or ICANS. B7-H3 overexpressed in >90% pediatric CNS tumors with minimal normal brain expression.
outcome_summary: 33% response, ICV delivery, 2 CR, repeated dosing feasible

title: DLL3-Targeted CAR-T in Relapsed/Refractory Small Cell Lung Cancer
text_summary: Phase I of DLL3-directed CAR-T with CXCR2 co-expression for enhanced solid tumor trafficking. 22 patients with ES-SCLC after platinum + checkpoint failure. ORR 27.3% (1 CR, 5 PR). DCR 63.6%. Median PFS 4.1 months, OS 8.2 months (vs 1.8/4.3 historical). CAR-T expansion correlated with response (5x higher Cmax in responders). CRS grade ≥3: 9%. DLL3 maintained in all progressors (TME-mediated resistance). DLL3 expressed >80% SCLC with minimal normal tissue.
outcome_summary: ORR 27.3%, DCR 63.6%, DLL3 retained, chemokine receptor trafficking

title: UCART123: Allogeneic Anti-CD123 CAR-T in Relapsed/Refractory AML
text_summary: UCART123 is allogeneic anti-CD123 CAR-T with TALEN-edited TCR knockout. Phase I dose escalation in 15 patients with R/R AML. CR/CRi 40% (6/15). Myelosuppression was significant: grade 4 neutropenia in 80%, reflecting on-target HSC toxicity from CD123 expression on normal progenitors. 4 patients bridged to allo-HSCT. CRS 60% (grade ≥3: 13%). Demonstrated that CD123 CAR-T can achieve remissions in AML but requires bridge-to-transplant strategy due to expected myeloablation.
outcome_summary: CR/CRi 40%, significant myelosuppression, bridge-to-transplant needed

title: CLL1-Targeted CAR-T for Relapsed/Refractory AML
text_summary: Phase I of CLL1 (CLEC12A)-directed CAR-T in 20 patients with R/R AML. CLL1 selected as target because expressed on leukemic stem cells but absent from normal HSCs (potentially safer than CD33/CD123). CR/CRi 55% (11/20). MRD negativity in 7 responders. Myelosuppression grade ≥3 in 35% (less than CD123 CAR-T). CRS 55% (grade ≥3: 5%). Monocytopenia expected (CLL1 on monocytes) but recovered by day 60. Median follow-up 12 months: 6/11 responders maintained CR without transplant.
outcome_summary: CR/CRi 55%, less myelosuppression than CD123, monocytopenia transient

title: CD7-Knockout Anti-CD7 CAR-T for Relapsed/Refractory T-ALL
text_summary: Phase I of CRISPR-edited CD7 CAR-T with CD7 gene disruption to prevent fratricide. 28 patients with R/R T-ALL (median age 14, median 3 prior lines). CD7 knockout efficiency 92-98%. CR/CRi 89% (25/28), MRD negativity 84% of responders. 12-month EFS 56%. Bridge to allo-HSCT in 64% of responders. Complete T-cell depletion (universal, requiring prophylaxis). CRS 75% (grade ≥3: 7%). Demonstrates gene-edited fratricide-resistant CAR-T achieves deep responses in T-ALL (5-year OS <10% with chemo).
outcome_summary: CR/CRi 89%, MRD neg 84%, fratricide-resistant via CD7 KO

title: GPC3-Targeted CAR-T for Advanced Hepatocellular Carcinoma: Phase II Expansion
text_summary: Phase II expansion of GPC3-CAR-T in 36 patients with advanced HCC (BCLC-C, Child-Pugh A/B7) after atezolizumab-bevacizumab + TKI failure. Hepatic artery infusion (HAI) delivery. ORR 36.1% by mRECIST (3 CR, 10 PR). DCR 69.4%. Median PFS 6.2 months. HAI achieved 10x higher intrahepatic CAR-T vs peripheral. Hepatotoxicity grade ≥3 in 22%. CRS 58% (grade ≥3: 8%). Regional delivery paradigm for liver-directed cellular therapy.
outcome_summary: ORR 36.1%, HAI delivery, 10x higher intrahepatic concentration

title: Mesothelin-Targeted Armored CAR-T (dnTGFbRII) in Malignant Pleural Mesothelioma
text_summary: Phase I of mesothelin-CAR-T co-expressing dominant-negative TGF-beta receptor II. 21 patients with unresectable mesothelioma. Intrapleural delivery + cyclophosphamide. ORR 43% by mRECIST (2 CR, 7 PR). Median PFS 5.8 months, 3 patients PFS >12 months. Tumor biopsies: dense CAR-T infiltration with retained effector function despite high TME TGF-beta. Pleural CRS 76% (manageable). Validates armored CAR-T for TME immunosuppression resistance.
outcome_summary: ORR 43%, armored dnTGFbRII, intrapleural delivery, retained function in TME

title: CD44v6-Targeted CAR-T in Relapsed/Refractory AML and Solid Tumors
text_summary: Phase I of CD44v6-directed CAR-T (MLM-CAR44.1) in 16 patients: 10 R/R AML, 6 solid tumors (HNSCC, pancreatic). CD44v6 expressed on AML blasts and epithelial cancers but also keratinocytes/monocytes. AML: CR/CRi 40% (4/10). Solid tumors: 1 PR, 3 SD. Skin toxicity (grade 2-3) in 44% reflecting keratinocyte expression — manageable with topical steroids. Monocytopenia in 56% (recovered). CRS 56% (grade ≥3: 6%). On-target/off-tumor skin toxicity was dose-limiting but reversible.
outcome_summary: AML CR/CRi 40%, skin toxicity 44% (keratinocyte expression), reversible

title: EGFRvIII-Targeted CAR-T for Recurrent Glioblastoma
text_summary: Phase I at University of Pennsylvania evaluating EGFRvIII-directed CAR-T in 10 patients with recurrent GBM. Single IV infusion without lymphodepletion. 1 patient had sustained regression for 18 months. Median OS 8.3 months. Post-treatment biopsies showed CAR-T infiltration but significant TME immunosuppression (increased Tregs, PD-L1 upregulation). EGFRvIII antigen loss detected in 5/7 evaluable patients at progression. Demonstrates antigen heterogeneity as key challenge in GBM and need for multi-antigen approaches.
outcome_summary: 1 sustained response, EGFRvIII loss in 5/7, TME immunosuppression

title: IL13Ra2-Targeted CAR-T Delivered Intracranially for Recurrent Glioblastoma
text_summary: Phase I at City of Hope evaluating intracranial delivery of IL13Rα2-CAR-T in recurrent GBM. 65 patients treated with ICV and/or intratumoral delivery. ORR 20% by RANO (3 CR, 10 PR). 1 patient had dramatic complete response lasting 7.5 months (published in NEJM 2016). Repeated dosing (up to 12 infusions) feasible and improved outcomes. No systemic CRS. Local CNS inflammation manageable. Demonstrates repeated intracranial dosing paradigm for CNS tumors.
outcome_summary: ORR 20%, 3 CR, repeated intracranial dosing feasible

title: PSMA-Targeted Armored CAR-T (dnTGFbRII) in Metastatic Castration-Resistant Prostate Cancer
text_summary: Phase I of PSMA-CAR-T co-expressing dominant-negative TGF-beta receptor in 24 patients with mCRPC after enzalutamide/abiraterone + docetaxel. PSA ≥50% decline in 33% (8/24).
